retrieved_docs.title: Median -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The median is the middle value in a set of numbers and is equivalent to the 50th percentile. In other words, the median is the midpoint of a set of numbers, with half of the values less than the median and half above it. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Morbidity And Mortality -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: On the other hand, mortality is related to the number of deaths caused by the health event under investigation. It can be communicated as a rate or as an absolute number. Mortality usually gets represented as a rate per 1000 individuals, also called the death rate. The calculation for this rate is to divide the number of deaths in a given time for a given population by the total population. To keep these values concise and for ease of comparison to other health events, this number can be multiplied by 1000 to reflect the “per 1000” rate of the target population.

retrieved_docs.title: Median -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: What should be done if the set contains an even number of values? In this case, the median is the average of the 2 middle numbers. To illustrate this principle, the second set of numbers (7, 3, 10, 2, 9, 2, 1, 4) should be considered. After arranging the set of numbers in ascending order, we get (1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 7, 9, 10). As the second set of numbers has 8 numbers, the 2 middle values are 3 and 4. The median is the average of these 2 numbers, which is calculated as follows: (3+4)/2 = 3.5 Notably, the median of 3.5 is different from the mean, or average, of the set of numbers, which is 4.75. This is calculated as follows: (1+2+2+3+4+7+9+10)/8 = 38/8 = 4.75

retrieved_docs.title: Fatal Accidents -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In any given day in the United States, 320 individuals die abruptly from accidental death. Worldwide in certain populations such as young men, this represents a major cause of death and in children age 0 to 4 years the most common cause of death, with asphyxiation leading the list of particular mechanisms. This activity summarizes accidental death across populations. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Effects on Child Health(Archived) -- Introduction -- Definition
retrieved_docs.content: The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends using BMI Z-score cut-offs of >1, > 2, and > 3 to define at risk of overweight, overweight, and obesity, respectively. [7] Z-score is measured in terms of standard deviations from the mean.

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the United States, stillbirth is generally defined as fetal death occurring at a minimum birth weight of 350 grams or a minimum gestational age of 20 weeks, with reporting requirements enforced at these thresholds. The WHO, however, defines stillbirth as fetal death at a minimum of 28 weeks' gestation. This distinction, along with some variation among states, may impact national and international comparisons of stillbirth rates. Standardization of the definition of stillbirth is a current priority. [1] "Stillbirth" has replaced "intrauterine fetal demise" as the terminology of choice based on the opinions of parent groups.

retrieved_docs.title: Median -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A short example will help clarify this point. In the set of 7 numbers (8, 6, 9, 5, 8, 23, 4), the median is found by first sorting the numbers from lowest to highest (in ascending order). The sorted set becomes (4, 5, 6, 8, 8, 9, 23). The median is the middle number. As there are 7 numbers in the set, the fourth value, '8', is the median. Notably, the median differs from the mean. The mean is the "average" of the values in the set, calculated as follows: (4+5+6+8+8+9+23)/7 = 63/7 = 9

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Morbidity And Mortality -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: From an etymological perspective, the word “epidemiology” can be divided into the Greek roots “epi,” “demos,” and “logos,” which respectively mean “upon,” “people,” and “the study of.” Historically, epidemiology has focused on population-level factors regarding communicable infectious diseases, but it has evolved to include non-communicable infectious diseases, chronic diseases, infant health, and environmental and behavioral health. Today, it is a wide-encapsulating umbrella that encompasses any health-related issues that may influence the overall health of a population, such as environmental exposures, injuries, natural disasters, and terrorism, to name a few. It is a multifaceted branch of medicine, fundamentally guided by systematic scientific inquiry via ratios, probabilities, and other statistical calculations, focusing on the incidence, distribution, and factors concerning diseases and health outcomes within a specific population.

retrieved_docs.title: Death Certification -- Other Issues -- Observation Period
retrieved_docs.content: The 2 examinations are separated by an observation period of: Twenty-four hours; for newborn up to 30 days Twelve hours for infants and children older than 30 days to 18 years [18] The first examination determines if the child has met all the required criteria for brain death. The second examination is to confirm brain death based on the fact that the condition is irreversible.

retrieved_docs.title: Young's Rule -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Young's Rule is an equation used to calculate pediatric medication dosage based on patient age and the known recommended adult dose. The definition of Young's Rule is the age of the patient divided by the age added to twelve, all multiplied by the recommended adult dose. This formula appears below:

retrieved_docs.title: Young's Rule -- Definition/Introduction -- [Age / (Age + 12)] x Recommended Adult Dose = Pediatric Dose
retrieved_docs.content: Young's Rule can be applied quickly in situations where the current weight of the patient is unknown. Other approaches to pediatric dosing using age include the Webster and Fried rules. [1] The Clark Rule or the Body Surface Area Rule can be implemented if the weight of the patient is known. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Algor Mortis -- Definition/Introduction -- Factors Affecting the Rate of Cooling
retrieved_docs.content: Age: Infants and the geriatric population generally lose body temperature more rapidly than middle-aged adults.

retrieved_docs.title: Bayley Scales Of Infant and Toddler Development -- Definition/Introduction -- Target Age Group
retrieved_docs.content: BSID III and BSID -4 are used for the age group starting from 16 days to 42 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Morbidity And Mortality -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Morbidity and mortality are two types of retrospective information that allows for continuous evaluation of the efficacy of either a specific health care system or an implemented intervention in place. For example, the use of maternal morbidity and mortality to gauge the risks of pregnancy and childbirth, as well as the efficacy of the health care they receive, are of vital importance. [2] In a related vein, accurate assessment of these measures are crucial to understanding and evaluating their impact and trajectory. Ultimately, mortality alongside morbidity allows epidemiologists to study further the burdens that a health event may place on a population. These metrics also allow stakeholders to more effectively prioritize which health events to tackle and allocate resources toward and proactively manage the potential onset of a health event.

retrieved_docs.title: Use Of The Child Pugh Score In Liver Disease -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Encephalopathy: None = 1 point, Grade 1 and 2 = 2 points, Grade 3 and 4 = 3 points Ascites:  None = 1 point, slight = 2 points, moderate = 3 points Bilirubin: under 2 mg/ml = 1 point, 2 to 3 mg/ml = 2 points, over 3 mg/ml = 3 points Albumin: greater than 3.5mg/ml = 1 point, 2.8 to 3.5mg/ml = 2 points, less than 2.8mg/ml = 3 points Prothrombin Time* (sec prolonged): less than 4 sec = 1 point, 4 to 6 sec = 2 points, over 6 sec = 3 points *Frequently INR will be used as a substitute for PT, with INR under 1.7 = 1 point, INR 1.7 to 2.2 = 2 points, INR above 2.2 = 3 points The severity of cirrhosis: Child-Pugh A: 5 to 6 points Child-Pugh B: 7 to 9 points Child-Pugh C: 10 to 15 points

retrieved_docs.title: Clark's Rule -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clark's rule is an equation used to calculate pediatric medication dosage based on the known weight of a patient and a known adult dose of medication to be used. Clark's rule equation is defined as the weight of the patient in pounds divided by the average standard weight of 150 pounds (68 kg) multiplied by the adult dose of a drug to obtain the pediatric medication dose, as is demonstrated below: (Weight* divided by 150 lbs.) x Adult Dose** = Pediatric Dosage (Weight*** divided by 68 kg) x Adult Dose** = Pediatric Dosage *Weight of pediatric patient in pounds (lbs.) **Adult dose is the recommended dosage for adult medication use ***Weight of pediatric patient in kilograms (kg)

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1983
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of “Duration of gestation” is classified within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM), indicating that gestational length is a recognized medical category in this coding system. Within this framework, the specific gestational interval of “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is linked to the outcome of a “liveborn” infant, reflecting that infants born after completing 29 to 30 weeks of pregnancy can be alive at birth. Accordingly, the “liveborn” outcome is treated as a distinct subclass or child concept under the broader umbrella of gestational duration, illustrating how gestational age ranges and birth outcomes are organized together in clinical classification and patient‑care documentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2523
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature, in turn, is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). وفاة حديث الولادة is identified as a concept within the Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology, indicating that neonatal death is recognized as a specific adverse event in pediatric care. Together, these relationships suggest that infants born preterm with low birth weight may develop short stature, which is linked to an increased risk of neonatal death, a critical adverse outcome captured by pediatric safety terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2850
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology incorporates the concept وفاة حديث الولادة, which denotes neonatal death, as a specific entry within its classification of pediatric adverse events. By including neonatal death in this terminology, healthcare providers can standardize reporting, track incidence, and implement targeted strategies to improve safety and outcomes for newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often develop reduced linear growth. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this vulnerable population may be linked to an increased risk of death shortly after birth. The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses adverse pediatric outcomes, and its subset includes the concepts تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse) and وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). Together, these relationships highlight a network of interrelated risk factors and adverse events: preterm low birth weight, short stature, and neonatal death are closely connected, while umbilical cord prolapse is also recognized within the same adverse‑event framework, underscoring the importance of comprehensive monitoring and intervention in high‑risk newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2329
retrieved_docs.content: Infancy is identified as a specific developmental stage that belongs to the broader category of the period of life between birth and death, situating this early phase within the overall lifespan continuum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight, early or threatened labor, threatened preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر), short stature (قصر القامة), and neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة) are interrelated clinical concepts. Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature, indicating that babies born prematurely with low weight are more likely to develop reduced adult height. Short stature itself is clinically associated with neonatal death, suggesting that children who remain short have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Early or threatened labor is used to classify threatened preterm labor, and threatened preterm labor is also clinically associated with short stature. Together, these relationships highlight that low birth weight and threatened preterm labor can contribute to short stature, which in turn is linked to higher neonatal mortality, underscoring the importance of monitoring growth and labor complications in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: The network links gestational timing, birth outcomes, and perinatal pathology through multiple classification systems. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is recorded as a **liveborn** outcome; liveborn is a subtype of **Duration of gestation**, which is coded within the **International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM)**. Within ICD‑10‑AM, **Duration of gestation** has the child concept **اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ** (“respiratory disorder”). The condition **Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation** (Arabic: تقييد النمو الجنيني and ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}) both classifies and is classified by **liveborn**, designating infants with these characteristics as a specific subgroup of liveborn births. This condition serves as the parent category for **Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96)**, which in turn includes two child entities:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2726
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology is a classification system that organizes adverse events occurring in children. Within this terminology, the specific concept of وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death) is included as a distinct adverse event. Additionally, the terminology encompasses other diseases of kidney and ureters, indicating that renal and ureteral conditions are represented as a subset of concepts within the system. By integrating both neonatal mortality and various kidney and ureter diseases, Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology provides a comprehensive framework for identifying, reporting, and managing a wide range of pediatric adverse events, thereby supporting improved patient safety and clinical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 699
retrieved_docs.content: the definition of liveborn status, and it is situated within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM), providing a coding framework for these concepts. Collectively, these hierarchical and classificatory relationships demonstrate how gestational age, birth weight, and fetal growth parameters intersect with broader perinatal disease categories, supporting clinical coding, epidemiologic surveillance, and comprehensive patient care for newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2503
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of “Duration of gestation” is organized within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM), which also encompasses conditions such as اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder). Within this classification framework, “liveborn” is regarded as a specific outcome associated with gestational duration; in particular, a gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed can result in a liveborn infant. This relationship positions liveborn as a concrete instance of the broader category of gestational duration outcomes, illustrating how gestational age, birth status, and disease classification are interconnected in medical documentation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 388
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period described as “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is classified under the Arabic term خديج (preterm birth) and is linked to a liveborn outcome. Preterm births (خديج) are frequently caused by متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). The concept of Duration of gestation is a key attribute of liveborn infants and is organized within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM). Within this classification system, respiratory disorders (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ) are listed as a child category, reflecting the close relationship between gestational age, preterm status, underlying pulmonary immaturity, and respiratory complications. Recognizing these interconnections and their coding in ICD‑10‑AM supports accurate diagnosis, documentation, and clinical management of infants born at 29–30 weeks gestation.
